name: Meade Lux Lewis
abstract: Anderson Meade "Lux" Lewis (September 4, 1905 – June 7, 1964) was an American pianist and composer, remembered for his playing in the boogie-woogie style. His best-known work, "Honky Tonk Train Blues", has been recorded by many artists.

concerts.label: Carnegie Pop Concert: Jazz at the Philharmonic
Carnegie Pop Concert: Jazz at the Philharmonic

about_quotes.speaker: Louie Bellson
about_quotes.evidence: Norman Granz called me and said, “I want you to do an album with Meade ‘Lux’ Lewis, just the two of you, piano and drums. That’s all.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Louie Bellson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Dick Hyman
about_quotes.evidence: I had grown up with a lot of blues and boogie woogie piano records by Meade Lux Lewis
about_quotes.doc_title: Dick Hyman interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clearwater Beach, Florida, March 17, 2001 [interview 2 of 2]

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: When I was workin' with Albert Ammons and Pete Johnson and Meade Lux Lewis, they done all the contract-signin'. I was just a...just the vocalist with the group.

about_quotes.speaker: Sammy Price
about_quotes.evidence: He said, 'No matter what you see me do, or what I do, don't you worry.' Do you know that he called at least a hundred piano players to play with me? James P. Johnson, Willie the Lion Smith, Donald Lambert , Meade Lux Lewis, Pete Johnson, da, da and da.

about_quotes.speaker: Maxine Sullivan
about_quotes.evidence: Meade 'Lux' Lewis stayed there on one of his engagements in New York. He stayed at the house and the night that he got killed he had just left from The House That Jazz Built.

about_quotes.speaker: Bill Coleman
about_quotes.evidence: Boogie Woogie was popular and John had put it together the three greatest exponents of that style of piano playing. They were Meade Lux Lewis, Pete Johnson and Albert Ammons.
